questions.question: What is gradient flow?
questions.answer: How gradients propagate backward through network during backpropagation. Good flow: gradients reach all layers effectively. Poor flow: vanishing/exploding gradients.

questions.question: How do skip connections improve gradient flow?
questions.answer: Provide direct paths for gradients to flow backward. Even if some layers have small gradients, skip connections maintain gradient magnitude throughout network.

questions.question: What types of normalization are used in deep learning?
questions.answer: Batch Normalization (across batch), Layer Normalization (across features), Instance Normalization (per sample), Group Normalization (feature groups).

questions.question: Why is normalization important in deep learning?
questions.answer: Stabilizes training, enables higher learning rates, reduces internal covariate shift, acts as regularization, improves gradient flow.

questions.question: What factors affect training stability?
questions.answer: Learning rate, initialization, normalization, gradient clipping, batch size, optimizer choice, architecture design, loss function.

questions.question: What are signs of unstable training?
questions.answer: Loss spikes or diverges, NaN/Inf values, oscillating metrics, exploding gradients, learning stagnation, mode collapse (GANs).

questions.question: List five techniques to prevent overfitting.
questions.answer: 1) Regularization (L1/L2), 2) Dropout, 3) Early stopping, 4) Data augmentation, 5) Cross-validation, 6) Reducing model complexity, 7) More training data.

questions.question: What are examples of sequential models?
questions.answer: RNNs, LSTMs, GRUs, Transformers, Temporal CNNs, WaveNet. Used for text, speech, time series, video - any sequential data.

questions.question: What is weighted aggregation in attention?
questions.answer: Computing output as weighted sum of values, where weights are attention scores. Output = Σ(attention_weights × values). Focuses on relevant parts.

questions.question: Why use multiple attention heads?
questions.answer: Each head can learn different aspects of relationships - syntax, semantics, position. Increases model capacity and representation diversity.

questions.question: How are outputs from multiple heads combined?
questions.answer: Concatenate outputs from all heads, then apply linear transformation to project back to model dimension.

questions.question: Why do Transformers need positional encoding?
questions.answer: Transformers have no inherent notion of sequence order (unlike RNNs). Positional encodings add position information so model knows word order.

questions.question: What are common positional encoding methods?
questions.answer: Sinusoidal (sin/cos functions of different frequencies), learned embeddings, relative position encodings, rotary embeddings (RoPE).

questions.question: Explain the Query-Key-Value mechanism.
questions.answer: Query: what we're looking for. Key: what to compare against. Value: actual content. Attention = softmax(Q·Kᵀ/√d)·V. Compute relevance, then aggregate values.

questions.question: How does masked language modeling work?
questions.answer: Randomly mask some tokens in input, train model to predict masked tokens using bidirectional context. BERT's main pre-training task.

questions.question: What percentage of tokens does BERT typically mask?
questions.answer: 15% of tokens. Of masked tokens: 80% replaced with [MASK], 10% random word, 10% unchanged. Prevents overfitting to [MASK] token.

questions.question: What is bidirectional context?
questions.answer: Using both left and right context simultaneously when processing each token. Sees full sentence context, unlike unidirectional (left-to-right only).

questions.question: What are autoregressive models?
questions.answer: Models that predict next element based on previous elements. P(x_t | x_1,...,x_{t-1}). Generate sequences left-to-right. Examples: GPT, language models.

questions.question: What is causal (masked) attention?
questions.answer: Attention that only looks at past tokens, not future ones. Prevents information leakage during training. Used in autoregressive models like GPT.

questions.question: What is pre-training in transfer learning?
questions.answer: Training model on large general dataset before fine-tuning on specific task. Learns general features/representations reusable across tasks.

questions.question: Why is pre-training effective?
questions.answer: Learns general patterns from massive data, provides good initialization, reduces need for task-specific data, improves performance and convergence speed.

questions.question: What learning rate should be used for fine-tuning?
questions.answer: Much lower than training from scratch (typically 1e-5 to 1e-4). Pre-trained weights already good, just need small adjustments for new task.

questions.question: What makes a good pre-trained model?
questions.answer: Trained on large diverse dataset, general enough to transfer, appropriate architecture, available checkpoints, well-documented, active community.

questions.question: What strategies exist for task adaptation?
questions.answer: Fine-tuning all layers, freezing early layers, progressive unfreezing, learning rate scheduling, task-specific heads, adapter layers.

questions.question: How are centroids initialized in K-Means?
questions.answer: Random: pick K random points. K-Means++: spread out initial centroids. Helps avoid bad local optima and speeds convergence.

questions.question: What is iterative optimization?
questions.answer: Repeatedly updating solution to improve objective. Each iteration moves toward better solution until convergence. Examples: gradient descent, K-Means, EM.

questions.question: What is a dendrogram?
questions.answer: Tree diagram showing hierarchical clustering results. Y-axis shows distance/similarity at which clusters merge. Cut at different heights for different number of clusters.

questions.question: How does density-based clustering work?
questions.answer: Groups points in high-density regions separated by low-density areas. Can find arbitrary shapes, identifies outliers. Example: DBSCAN.

questions.question: Name five common probability distributions in ML.
questions.answer: Normal/Gaussian, Bernoulli, Binomial, Poisson, Exponential, Beta, Dirichlet, Categorical, Uniform.

questions.question: Explain the E-step and M-step in EM algorithm.
questions.answer: E-step: compute expected values of latent variables given current parameters. M-step: update parameters to maximize expected likelihood. Iterate until convergence.

questions.question: What are latent variables?
questions.answer: Hidden/unobserved variables that influence observed data. Not directly measured but inferred. Examples: cluster assignments in GMM, topics in LDA.

questions.question: What do eigenvalues represent in PCA?
questions.answer: Amount of variance explained by each principal component. Larger eigenvalue = more important component. Used to determine number of components to keep.

questions.question: What do eigenvectors represent in PCA?
questions.answer: Directions of principal components - axes of maximum variance. Orthogonal to each other. Data projected onto these directions.

questions.question: What are common matrix decomposition methods?
questions.answer: Eigendecomposition, SVD (Singular Value Decomposition), QR decomposition, LU decomposition, Cholesky decomposition.

questions.question: What does correlation measure?
questions.answer: Linear relationship between two variables. Range: [-1, 1]. 1: perfect positive, -1: perfect negative, 0: no linear relationship. Pearson, Spearman, Kendall.

questions.question: Why is dimensionality reduction important?
questions.answer: Reduces curse of dimensionality, improves computational efficiency, removes noise, enables visualization, addresses multicollinearity, reduces storage.

questions.question: What is manifold learning?
questions.answer: Assumption that high-dimensional data lies on lower-dimensional manifold (curved surface). Methods: t-SNE, UMAP, Isomap learn this manifold structure.

questions.question: What is topological data analysis?
questions.answer: Studying shape and structure of data using topology. Finds holes, connected components, clusters at multiple scales. Used in UMAP, persistent homology.

questions.question: What is encoder-decoder architecture?
questions.answer: Encoder compresses input to latent representation. Decoder reconstructs or generates output from latent. Used in autoencoders, seq2seq, transformers.

questions.question: What is latent space?
questions.answer: Lower-dimensional representation learned by encoder. Captures essential features. Smooth latent spaces enable interpolation and generation.

questions.question: What is KL divergence?
questions.answer: Kullback-Leibler divergence measures difference between two probability distributions. KL(P||Q) = Σ P(x)log(P(x)/Q(x)). Non-negative, non-symmetric.

questions.question: How is KL divergence used in VAEs?
questions.answer: VAE loss = reconstruction loss + KL(q(z|x) || p(z)). KL term regularizes latent space to match prior (typically standard normal).

questions.question: How is a GAN generator trained?
questions.answer: Receives gradients through discriminator. Tries to maximize discriminator's error. Loss encourages generating realistic samples that fool discriminator.

questions.question: How is a GAN discriminator trained?
questions.answer: Binary classification: real (1) vs fake (0). Maximizes probability of correct classification. Provides learning signal to generator.

questions.question: What is adversarial training?
questions.answer: Two models compete: generator creates, discriminator judges. Minimax game reaches Nash equilibrium where generator produces realistic data.

questions.question: What are the main types of generative models?
questions.answer: GANs (adversarial), VAEs (variational), Autoregressive models (PixelCNN, GPT), Flow-based (normalizing flows), Diffusion models.

questions.question: What is a state in reinforcement learning?
questions.answer: Complete description of agent's situation. Contains all information needed to make decisions. Can be fully observable (MDP) or partial (POMDP).

questions.question: What are actions in reinforcement learning?
questions.answer: Choices available to agent in each state. Can be discrete (finite set) or continuous. Agent selects actions to maximize cumulative reward.

questions.question: How should rewards be designed?
questions.answer: Should reflect true objective, frequent enough for learning, not too sparse, avoid reward hacking, consider delayed rewards, balance exploration.

questions.question: What are state transitions?
questions.answer: Changes from one state to another based on action taken. Can be deterministic or stochastic. Defined by transition function P(s'|s,a).

questions.question: What is the difference between state-value and action-value functions?
questions.answer: State-value V(s): expected return from state following policy. Action-value Q(s,a): expected return from state, taking action, then following policy.

questions.question: What is the Bellman equation?
questions.answer: Recursive relationship for value functions. V(s) = maxₐ[R(s,a) + γΣP(s'|s,a)V(s')]. Expresses value as immediate reward plus discounted future value.

questions.question: What is experience replay?
questions.answer: Storing past experiences (s,a,r,s') in buffer and sampling randomly for training. Breaks correlation between consecutive samples, improves stability, enables reuse.

questions.question: What is policy optimization?
questions.answer: Directly optimizing policy parameters to maximize expected return. Methods: REINFORCE, PPO, TRPO. Alternative to value-based methods.

questions.question: How does gradient ascent differ from gradient descent?
questions.answer: Ascent maximizes objective by moving in gradient direction. Descent minimizes by moving opposite gradient. Used in policy gradient to maximize reward.

questions.question: What types of policies exist?
questions.answer: Deterministic: π(s) = a. Stochastic: π(a|s) = probability. On-policy: evaluate policy being followed. Off-policy: evaluate different policy.

questions.question: How does domain knowledge help in ML?
questions.answer: Guides feature engineering, informs model selection, helps interpret results, identifies important variables, designs better architectures, improves data collection.

questions.question: What are essential data preprocessing steps?
questions.answer: Handling missing values, outlier treatment, scaling/normalization, encoding categoricals, feature engineering, data cleaning, splitting datasets.

questions.question: What is the Min-Max scaling formula?
questions.answer: x_scaled = (x - min) / (max - min). Scales to [0,1]. For custom range [a,b]: x_scaled = a + (x-min)(b-a)/(max-min).

questions.question: What is Z-score standardization?
questions.answer: z = (x - μ) / σ. Centers data at 0 with standard deviation 1. More robust to outliers than Min-Max. Assumes roughly normal distribution.

questions.question: Why are mean and standard deviation important?
questions.answer: Mean: central tendency. Std: spread/variability. Together describe distribution. Used in normalization, initialization, batch norm, statistical tests.

questions.question: What are ordinal vs nominal categorical variables?
questions.answer: Ordinal: have meaningful order (low/medium/high). Nominal: no inherent order (colors, countries). Different encoding strategies for each.

questions.question: What metrics evaluate model performance?
questions.answer: Classification: accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUC. Regression: MSE, RMSE, MAE, R². Also: confusion matrix, ROC curve, learning curves.

questions.question: What are the main challenges in classification?
questions.answer: Class imbalance, overlapping classes, outliers, high dimensionality, non-linear boundaries, interpretability vs accuracy tradeoff.

questions.question: What is a true positive?
questions.answer: Correctly predicted positive case. Actually positive and predicted positive. Upper-left cell in binary confusion matrix.

questions.question: What is a false positive and why is it problematic?
questions.answer: Predicting positive when actually negative (Type I error). Problems: unnecessary treatments, false alarms, wasted resources, reduced trust.

questions.question: When should you use different classification metrics?
questions.answer: Balanced classes: accuracy. Imbalanced: F1, precision, recall. Cost-sensitive: weighted metrics. Multiple classes: macro/micro averaging, confusion matrix.

questions.question: What is harmonic mean and why use it for F1?
questions.answer: HM = n/(Σ1/xᵢ). Heavily penalizes small values. For F1: balances precision and recall - if either is low, F1 is low.

questions.question: What is True Positive Rate?
questions.answer: TPR = TP/(TP+FN) = Recall = Sensitivity. Proportion of actual positives correctly identified. Y-axis of ROC curve.

questions.question: What is False Positive Rate?
questions.answer: FPR = FP/(FP+TN). Proportion of actual negatives incorrectly predicted as positive. X-axis of ROC curve. Want to minimize.

questions.question: When is regression appropriate?
questions.answer: When predicting continuous numerical values: prices, temperatures, probabilities, counts. Target variable is quantitative, not categorical.

questions.question: Compare MAE vs MSE as error metrics.
questions.answer: MAE: average absolute error, robust to outliers, same units as target. MSE: penalizes large errors more, differentiable everywhere, amplifies outliers.

questions.question: What does variance explained mean?
questions.answer: Proportion of total variance in target captured by model. R² = 1 - (SS_res/SS_tot). 1 = perfect, 0 = no better than mean.

questions.question: What factors influence model selection?
questions.answer: Problem type, data size, interpretability needs, computational resources, deployment constraints, accuracy requirements, available features.

questions.question: What is exhaustive search?
questions.answer: Trying all possible combinations. Guarantees finding best solution in search space but computationally expensive. Grid search is example.

questions.question: What are advantages of random sampling in hyperparameter tuning?
questions.answer: More efficient than grid search, explores wider range, can find good solutions faster, works better in high dimensions, easy to parallelize.

questions.question: How does Bayesian statistics differ from frequentist?
questions.answer: Bayesian: treats parameters as random variables with distributions, uses prior knowledge, updates beliefs with data. Frequentist: parameters are fixed, uses p-values/confidence intervals.

questions.question: What are surrogate models in Bayesian optimization?
questions.answer: Cheap-to-evaluate models (typically Gaussian processes) that approximate expensive objective function. Guide search by predicting performance.

questions.question: What indicates convergence in training?
questions.answer: Loss plateaus, validation metrics stable, gradients small, parameters stop changing significantly, early stopping criterion met.

questions.question: What are learning rate warm-up and cool-down?
questions.answer: Warm-up: gradually increase LR at start to stabilize training. Cool-down/decay: reduce LR toward end for fine-tuning. Improves convergence.

questions.question: What are adaptive optimization
Why is programming essential for machine learning?
questions.answer: Programming enables implementing algorithms, processing data, training models, experimenting with hyperparameters, and deploying solutions. It's the practical tool that brings ML theory to life.

questions.question: What are the most popular programming languages for machine learning?
questions.answer: Python (most popular, rich libraries), R (statistics-focused), Julia (high-performance), Java/Scala (production systems), C++ (performance-critical applications).

questions.question: What mathematical operations are fundamental to machine learning?
questions.answer: Arithmetic operations, exponentiation, logarithms, summation, products, basic algebra, equations solving, and understanding of functions.

questions.question: Why is mathematical maturity important for machine learning?
questions.answer: ML relies heavily on mathematical concepts - understanding algorithms, deriving methods, interpreting results, and debugging models all require solid mathematical foundations.

questions.question: What is a derivative and why is it important in ML?
questions.answer: Derivative measures rate of change of a function. In ML, derivatives (gradients) show how to adjust parameters to minimize loss, forming the basis of optimization.

questions.question: What is the derivative of f(x) = x²?
questions.answer: f'(x) = 2x. Using power rule: d/dx[xⁿ] = n·xⁿ⁻¹

questions.question: What is an integral and where is it used in ML?
questions.answer: Integral computes area under a curve or accumulation. In ML, used in probability (normalizing distributions), computing expectations, and continuous optimization.

questions.question: What is a partial derivative?
questions.answer: Derivative with respect to one variable while holding others constant. For f(x,y), ∂f/∂x measures change in f as x varies with y fixed.

questions.question: Why are partial derivatives crucial for neural networks?
questions.answer: Neural networks have many parameters. Partial derivatives show how each parameter affects loss independently, enabling gradient-based optimization of all parameters.

questions.question: What are the basic matrix operations used in ML?
questions.answer: Addition, subtraction, scalar multiplication, matrix multiplication, transpose, inverse, determinant, eigendecomposition.

questions.question: Why is matrix multiplication non-commutative?
questions.answer: AXB is not equal to BXA in general because dimensions must align specific way and the operation combines rows of first matrix with columns of second.

questions.question: What is a vector space?
questions.answer: A set of vectors where vector addition and scalar multiplication are defined, satisfying properties like associativity, commutativity, distributivity, and existence of zero vector.

questions.question: Why are vector spaces important in machine learning?
questions.answer: Data is represented as vectors in feature spaces. Understanding vector spaces helps with dimensionality reduction, feature transformations, and geometric interpretations of algorithms.

questions.question: What are the basic set operations?
questions.answer: Union , intersection , complement, difference, subset, superset, empty set, cardinality.

questions.question: How is set theory used in probability?
questions.answer: Events are represented as sets. Set operations correspond to probability operations: union (OR), intersection (AND), complement (NOT). Sample space is universal set.

questions.question: What is conditional probability?
questions.answer: P(A given B) = P(A and B)/P(B). Probability of A given B has occurred. Updates probability based on new information.

questions.question: Give a real-world example of conditional probability.
questions.answer: P(disease|positive test) - probability of having disease given positive test result. Different from P(disease) without test information.

questions.question: Why do we split data into training and testing sets?
questions.answer: Training set: learn patterns. Testing set: evaluate generalization to unseen data. Prevents overfitting assessment - model hasn't seen test data during training.

questions.question: What is a typical train-test split ratio?
questions.answer: Common ratios: 80-20, 70-30, 60-20-20 (train-validation-test). Depends on dataset size - larger datasets can afford smaller test percentages.

questions.question: What is data leakage between training and testing?
questions.answer: When test data information influences training (e.g., normalization using full dataset). Causes overly optimistic performance estimates. Must keep sets strictly separate.

questions.question: What factors contribute to model complexity?
questions.answer: Number of parameters, depth (layers), width (neurons per layer), non-linearity degree, feature interactions, architectural choices.

questions.question: How does model complexity relate to the bias-variance tradeoff?
questions.answer: Low complexity: high bias (underfitting), low variance. High complexity: low bias, high variance (overfitting). Optimal complexity balances both.

questions.question: What is generalization in machine learning?
questions.answer: Model's ability to perform well on unseen data, not just training data. Good generalization means learning patterns, not memorizing examples.

questions.question: What techniques improve generalization?
questions.answer: Regularization, cross-validation, dropout, data augmentation, early stopping, ensemble methods, proper train-test splits, sufficient training data.

questions.question: Why is model evaluation important?
questions.answer: Determines if model meets requirements, compares different models, identifies weaknesses, guides improvements, ensures reliability before deployment.

questions.question: What's the difference between validation and test sets?
questions.answer: Validation: tune hyperparameters, used during development. Test: final unbiased evaluation, used once at end. Test set should never influence model development.

questions.question: What is stratified sampling in data splitting?
questions.answer: Maintaining class distribution proportions across splits. Ensures each split is representative, especially important for imbalanced datasets.

questions.question: Why is random splitting important?
questions.answer: Prevents ordering bias - if data is sorted by class or time, non-random splits could give unrepresentative sets leading to misleading evaluation.

questions.question: What is information theory's role in machine learning?
questions.answer: Provides framework for quantifying information and uncertainty. Key concepts: entropy (uncertainty), mutual information (shared information), KL divergence (distribution difference).

questions.question: What does Shannon entropy measure?
questions.answer: Average information content or uncertainty in a random variable. H(X) = -Σ P(x)log₂P(x). Higher entropy = more uncertainty/randomness.

questions.question: Why are logarithms important in machine learning?
questions.answer: Log-likelihood for probability optimization, entropy calculations, log-loss, transforming skewed features, numerical stability (prevents underflow), growth rate analysis.

questions.question: What is log(a  multiply b)?
questions.answer: log(a multiply b) = log(a) + log(b). Logarithm converts multiplication to addition, useful for numerical stability with very small probabilities.

questions.question: What is model aggregation in ensemble learning?
questions.answer: Combining predictions from multiple models to produce final output. Methods: averaging (regression), voting (classification), weighted combinations, stacking.

questions.question: Why does aggregating models often improve performance?
questions.answer: Different models make different errors. Averaging reduces variance, combining diverse models reduces overall error through wisdom of crowds effect.

questions.question: What are common sampling methods in machine learning?
questions.answer: Random sampling, stratified sampling, bootstrap sampling, importance sampling, systematic sampling, cluster sampling.

questions.question: When would you use stratified sampling?
questions.answer: When classes are imbalanced or you want to ensure all groups are represented proportionally in sample. Maintains distribution of important strata.

questions.question: What techniques reduce variance in ML models?
questions.answer: Bagging/ensemble methods, increasing training data, regularization, reducing model complexity, cross-validation, feature selection, dropout.

questions.question: Why is variance reduction important?
questions.answer: High variance causes overfitting - model too sensitive to training data noise. Reducing variance improves generalization to new data.

questions.question: What is sequential learning?
questions.answer: Learning where order matters - each model learns from or builds upon previous ones. Examples: boosting, online learning, curriculum learning.

questions.question: How does sequential learning differ from parallel learning?
questions.answer: Sequential: models trained one after another, each using info from previous (boosting). Parallel: models trained independently simultaneously (bagging).

questions.question: What is a weak learner?
questions.answer: A model slightly better than random guessing (>50% accuracy for binary). Simple, low-variance models like shallow decision trees, stumps.

questions.question: Why are weak learners useful in boosting?
questions.answer: Many weak learners combined sequentially create strong learner. Each corrects previous mistakes. Prevents overfitting while building complex models.

questions.question: What are weighted samples in machine learning?
questions.answer: Assigning different importance to training examples. Higher weights mean model focuses more on those examples during training.

questions.question: How does AdaBoost use sample weights?
questions.answer: Increases weights of misclassified examples after each iteration. Next model focuses on hard examples. Correctly classified examples get lower weights.

questions.question: What is exponential loss?
questions.answer: L(y,f(x)) = exp(-y·f(x)) where y∈{-1,1}. Used in AdaBoost. Penalizes misclassifications exponentially, focusing heavily on hard examples.

questions.question: Why does AdaBoost use exponential loss?
questions.answer: Makes mathematical optimization tractable, naturally up-weights misclassified examples exponentially, leads to margin-maximizing properties.

questions.question: What is residual fitting in gradient boosting?
questions.answer: Training each new model to predict residuals (errors) of previous models. Gradually corrects mistakes by learning what previous ensemble got wrong.

questions.question: Why is fitting residuals effective?
questions.answer: Directly targets model weaknesses. Each iteration reduces remaining error. Eventually, sum of predictions approximates true target very well.

questions.question: What is tree pruning?
questions.answer: Removing branches from decision tree that provide little predictive power. Reduces complexity and overfitting while maintaining performance.

questions.question: What are pre-pruning and post-pruning?
questions.answer: Pre-pruning: stop growing tree early (max depth, min samples). Post-pruning: grow full tree then remove branches. Post-pruning often better but more expensive.

questions.question: What is histogram-based learning in LightGBM?
questions.answer: Bucketing continuous features into discrete bins (histograms). Reduces complexity, speeds up training, decreases memory usage, handles sparse data efficiently.

questions.question: What is leaf-wise growth in LightGBM?
questions.answer: Growing tree by splitting leaf with maximum loss reduction, rather than level-wise. More asymmetric trees, faster training, but more prone to overfitting.

questions.question: How does leaf-wise differ from level-wise growth?
questions.answer: Leaf-wise: split best leaf regardless of depth. Level-wise: complete each level before next. Leaf-wise faster and more accurate but needs depth limits.

questions.question: What are categorical features?
questions.answer: Features with discrete, non-numeric values representing categories (colors, brands, countries). Require special encoding for ML algorithms.

questions.question: How does CatBoost handle categorical features?
questions.answer: Uses ordered target statistics - computes target statistics for categories using previous rows only. Prevents target leakage, no need for manual encoding.

questions.question: What is ordered boosting in CatBoost?
questions.answer: Trains models on permuted data to prevent overfitting from target leakage. Each example gets prediction from model trained without that example.

questions.question: How is geometry used in machine learning?
questions.answer: Feature spaces are geometric, distances measure similarity, hyperplanes separate classes, manifolds represent data structure, angles measure correlation.

questions.question: What is a hyperplane?
questions.answer: A flat subspace of n-1 dimensions in n-dimensional space. In 2D: line. In 3D: plane. In higher dimensions: generalized plane separating space.

questions.question: What is the margin in SVM?
questions.answer: Distance between decision boundary (hyperplane) and nearest data points from each class. SVM maximizes this margin for better generalization.

questions.question: What is feature mapping?
questions.answer: Transforming features from original space to new space. φ: X → F. Makes data linearly separable in new space, enables non-linear models.

questions.question: What is an inner product?
questions.answer: Generalization of dot product: ⟨x,y⟩. Measures similarity/projection. In Euclidean space: x·y = Σxᵢyᵢ. Used in kernel methods.

questions.question: What is a Gaussian function?
questions.answer: Bell-shaped curve: f(x) = a·exp(-(x-b)²/(2c²)). Also called normal/bell curve. Basis for RBF kernels, probability distributions.

questions.question: What is instance-based learning?
questions.answer: Learning that stores training instances and uses them directly for prediction. No explicit model building. Examples: KNN, case-based reasoning.

questions.question: What are vector norms and why are they important?
questions.answer: Norms measure vector magnitude/length. L1 (Manhattan): Σ|xᵢ|. L2 (Euclidean): √(Σxᵢ²). Used in regularization, distance metrics, normalization.

questions.question: What is the dot product?
questions.answer: x·y = Σxᵢyᵢ = |x||y|cos(θ). Measures similarity, projection. Returns scalar from two vectors. Zero dot product means orthogonal vectors.

questions.question: What is conditional independence?
questions.answer: X and Y are conditionally independent given Z if P(X,Y|Z) = P(X|Z)P(Y|Z). Knowing Z makes X and Y independent.

questions.question: What is binary classification?
questions.answer: Classification with two classes (positive/negative, yes/no, 0/1). Simplest classification task. Foundation for multi-class methods.

questions.question: What does linearly separable mean?
questions.answer: Classes can be perfectly separated by a line (2D), plane (3D), or hyperplane (higher dimensions). Perceptron only works for linearly separable data.

questions.question: What are hidden layers in neural networks?
questions.answer: Layers between input and output. Extract progressively abstract features. More hidden layers = deeper network = more complex patterns learnable.

questions.question: Why is non-linearity crucial in neural networks?
questions.answer: Without non-linear activations, multiple layers collapse to single linear transformation. Non-linearity enables learning complex patterns and arbitrary functions.

questions.question: What function properties are important in ML?
questions.answer: Continuity (smooth optimization), differentiability (gradients exist), convexity (global optimum), monotonicity, boundedness, smoothness.

questions.question: What is a piecewise function?
questions.answer: Function defined by different expressions in different intervals. Example: ReLU is piecewise - max(0,x) = {x if x>0, 0 if x≤0}.

questions.question: What is the tanh (hyperbolic tangent) function?
questions.answer: tanh(x) = (eˣ - e⁻ˣ)/(eˣ + e⁻ˣ). Range: (-1,1). Similar to sigmoid but zero-centered. Used as activation function.

questions.question: What is a probability distribution?
questions.answer: Function describing probability of different outcomes. Discrete: PMF. Continuous: PDF. Must sum/integrate to 1. Examples: Normal, Bernoulli, Uniform.

questions.question: How does multi-class classification differ from binary?
questions.answer: More than two classes to predict. Requires different output layer (softmax), loss function (categorical cross-entropy), and strategies (one-vs-rest, one-vs-one).

questions.question: What are the requirements for matrix multiplication?
questions.answer: For A multiply B: number of columns in A must equal number of rows in B. Resulting matrix has A's rows and B's columns.

questions.question: Why do deep learning models need large datasets?
questions.answer: Many parameters to learn, prevents overfitting, captures diverse patterns, enables learning complex representations, improves generalization.

questions.question: What are basic image processing operations?
questions.answer: Filtering, convolution, edge detection, smoothing, sharpening, resizing, normalization, augmentation, color space conversion.

questions.question: What parameters define a convolution operation?
questions.answer: Kernel/filter size, stride (step size), padding (border handling), number of filters, dilation rate.

questions.question: What do filters detect in CNNs?
questions.answer: Early layers: edges, colors, textures. Middle layers: patterns, shapes. Deep layers: complex objects, parts. Learned automatically during training.

questions.question: How does signal processing relate to deep learning?
questions.answer: Convolution comes from signal processing. Fourier transforms, filtering, spectral analysis. Used in audio processing, time series, CNNs.

questions.question: What is downsampling?
questions.answer: Reducing spatial dimensions of feature maps. Methods: pooling (max/average), strided convolutions. Reduces computation, provides translation invariance.

questions.question: What is feature extraction?
questions.answer: Transforming raw data into meaningful features. CNNs extract features automatically. Traditional: SIFT, HOG. Modern: learned representations.

questions.question: What is sequential data?
questions.answer: Data where order matters - time series, text, speech, video. Has temporal or positional dependencies. Requires special architectures (RNN, LSTM, Transformers).

questions.question: What are temporal dependencies?
questions.answer: Relationships between data points across time. Past influences future. Examples: word context in sentences, stock price trends, video frame sequences.

questions.question: What are gating mechanisms in RNNs?
questions.answer: Learnable gates that control information flow. Decide what to remember, forget, or output. Used in LSTM/GRU to handle long-term dependencies.

questions.question: Why is feature scaling important in machine learning?
questions.answer: Many algorithms (KNN, SVM, neural networks, gradient descent) are sensitive to feature magnitudes. Scaling ensures features contribute equally, speeds up convergence, and improves numerical stability.

questions.question: What is the difference between normalization and standardization?
questions.answer: Normalization (Min-Max scaling) scales to [0,1]: (x-min)/(max-min). Standardization (Z-score) scales to mean=0, std=1: (x-mean)/std. Standardization is more robust to outliers.

questions.question: Which algorithms are sensitive to feature scaling?
questions.answer: Distance-based: KNN, K-Means, SVM. Gradient descent-based: Linear/Logistic Regression, Neural Networks. Not sensitive: Tree-based algorithms (Random Forest, XGBoost).

questions.question: When would you use Min-Max scaling vs Z-score standardization?
questions.answer: Min-Max: when you need bounded range [0,1], no outliers, neural networks with sigmoid/tanh. Z-score: when data has outliers, Gaussian distribution, comparing different scales.

questions.question: What is one-hot encoding and when is it used?
questions.answer: Converting categorical variables into binary vectors where only one element is 1 (hot) and others are 0. Used when categories have no ordinal relationship.

questions.question: Transform the categorical variable ['red', 'blue', 'green'] using one-hot encoding.
questions.answer: red: [1,0,0], blue: [0,1,0], green: [0,0,1]. Each category becomes a binary column.

questions.question: What is the dummy variable trap?
questions.answer: Perfect multicollinearity when all dummy variables are included. If you have n categories, use n-1 dummies since the nth can be inferred. Important for linear regression.

questions.question: What are alternatives to one-hot encoding for categorical variables?
questions.answer: Label encoding (ordinal), target encoding (mean of target), frequency encoding, binary encoding, hash encoding, embeddings (for high cardinality).

questions.question: What is dropout in neural networks?
questions.answer: Regularization technique that randomly drops (sets to zero) a fraction of neurons during training. Each training iteration uses a different subset of the network.

questions.question: How does dropout help prevent overfitting?
questions.answer: Forces network to learn robust features that work with different neuron combinations. Prevents co-adaptation where neurons rely too heavily on specific other neurons. Acts like ensemble of networks.

questions.question: What happens to dropout during inference/testing?
questions.answer: Dropout is turned off - all neurons are active. Outputs are scaled by the dropout rate to account for more active neurons than during training.

questions.question: What is an appropriate dropout rate to use?
questions.answer: Typically 0.2-0.5 for hidden layers, 0.5 is common default. Input layers use lower rates (0.1-0.2). Higher rates for larger networks prone to overfitting.

questions.question: What is batch normalization?
questions.answer: Normalizing layer inputs by subtracting batch mean and dividing by batch standard deviation. Includes learnable scale and shift parameters. Applied to mini-batches during training.

questions.question: What problems does batch normalization solve?
questions.answer: Internal covariate shift (changing distributions during training), vanishing/exploding gradients, allows higher learning rates, reduces sensitivity to initialization, provides regularization effect.

questions.question: How does batch normalization affect training speed?
questions.answer: Significantly accelerates training by allowing higher learning rates, reducing sensitivity to initialization, and stabilizing gradient flow. Can enable 10x faster training.

questions.question: Where in a neural network layer is batch normalization typically applied?
questions.answer: After linear transformation but before activation function: x → Linear → BatchNorm → Activation. Though some research suggests after activation works too.

questions.question: What is transfer learning?
questions.answer: Using knowledge from a model trained on one task to improve performance on a different but related task. Leverages pre-trained models instead of training from scratch.

questions.question: When is transfer learning useful?
questions.answer: When you have limited labeled data for your task, similar tasks exist with large datasets, computational resources are limited, or you want to improve performance and reduce training time.

questions.question: What is the difference between feature extraction and fine-tuning in transfer learning?
questions.answer: Feature extraction: freeze pre-trained layers, only train new top layers. Fine-tuning: unfreeze some/all pre-trained layers and train them with low learning rate on new task.

questions.question: Give examples of pre-trained models commonly used for transfer learning.
questions.answer: Vision: ResNet, VGG, Inception, EfficientNet. NLP: BERT, GPT, RoBERTa, T5. Audio: wav2vec. General: ImageNet-pretrained CNNs, transformer-based language models.

questions.question: What is an autoencoder?
questions.answer: Unsupervised neural network that learns to compress data into lower-dimensional representation (encoding) and reconstruct it (decoding). Trained to minimize reconstruction error.

questions.question: What are the two main components of an autoencoder?
questions.answer: Encoder: compresses input to latent representation. Decoder: reconstructs input from latent representation. Connected through bottleneck layer.

questions.question: What is the bottleneck layer in an autoencoder?
questions.answer: The middle layer with lowest dimensionality that contains compressed representation of input. Forces network to learn efficient encoding by limiting information capacity.

questions.question: What are some applications of autoencoders?
questions.answer: Dimensionality reduction, denoising, anomaly detection, data compression, feature learning, image generation, data augmentation, pretraining for supervised tasks.

questions.question: What are the two networks in a GAN?
questions.answer: Generator: creates fake data from random noise. Discriminator: distinguishes between real and fake data. They compete in a minimax game.

questions.question: Explain the adversarial training process in GANs.
questions.answer: Generator tries to fool discriminator by creating realistic fake data. Discriminator tries to correctly identify real vs fake. They improve iteratively through competition until generator produces highly realistic data.

questions.question: What is the role of the generator in a GAN?
questions.answer: Takes random noise as input and transforms it into synthetic data (images, text, etc.) that mimics the real data distribution. Goal is to fool the discriminator.

questions.question: What is the role of the discriminator in a GAN?
questions.answer: Binary classifier that predicts whether input is real (from training data) or fake (from generator). Provides feedback signal to train the generator.

questions.question: What are common challenges in training GANs?
questions.answer: Mode collapse (generator produces limited variety), vanishing gradients, instability/oscillation, difficult hyperparameter tuning, sensitive to architecture choices, hard to evaluate.

questions.question: What is Q-learning?
questions.answer: Model-free reinforcement learning algorithm that learns the value (Q-value) of taking each action in each state. Aims to learn optimal policy by estimating action-value function.

questions.question: What does the Q in Q-learning represent?
questions.answer: Q stands for Quality. Q(s,a) represents the expected cumulative reward of taking action a in state s and following optimal policy thereafter.

questions.question: Explain the Q-learning update rule.
questions.answer: Q(s,a) ← Q(s,a) + α[r + γ max Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]. α: learning rate, r: reward, γ: discount factor, s': next state. Updates based on TD error.

questions.question: What is the difference between on-policy and off-policy learning?
questions.answer: On-policy: learns about the policy being followed (e.g., SARSA). Off-policy: learns about optimal policy while following different behavior policy (e.g., Q-learning). Off-policy can learn from exploratory actions.

questions.question: What is a Markov Decision Process?
questions.answer: Mathematical framework for modeling decision-making where outcomes are partly random and partly controllable. Consists of states, actions, transition probabilities, and rewards.

questions.question: What are the components of an MDP?
questions.answer: States (S): possible situations. Actions (A): available choices. Transition function P(s'|s,a): probability of next state. Reward function R(s,a): immediate reward. Discount factor γ.

questions.question: What is the Markov property?
questions.answer: Future state depends only on current state and action, not on history. P(s_{t+1}|s_t,a_t,s_{t-1},...) = P(s_{t+1}|s_t,a_t). Past is irrelevant given present.

questions.question: What is a policy in the context of MDPs?
questions.answer: A policy π is a strategy that maps states to actions: π(s) = a or π(a|s) = probability. Defines agent's behavior. Goal is to find optimal policy maximizing expected cumulative reward.

questions.question: What is ensemble learning?
questions.answer: Combining multiple models to create a stronger learner. Leverages diversity among models to reduce errors and improve generalization through wisdom of crowds.

questions.question: Why do ensemble methods often outperform single models?
questions.answer: Individual models make different errors. Averaging reduces variance, combining diverse models reduces bias, and ensemble is more robust to noise and outliers than any single model.

questions.question: What are the main types of ensemble methods?
questions.answer: Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating, e.g., Random Forest), Boosting (sequential learning, e.g., AdaBoost, XGBoost), Stacking (meta-learning), Voting (majority/average).

questions.question: What is the difference between bagging and boosting?
questions.answer: Bagging: parallel, independent models on bootstrap samples, reduces variance. Boosting: sequential, each model corrects previous errors, reduces bias. Bagging for high variance, boosting for high bias.

questions.question: What is the difference between parameters and hyperparameters?
questions.answer: Parameters: learned from data (weights, biases). Hyperparameters: set before training, control learning process (learning rate, number of layers, regularization strength).

questions.question: What is grid search?
questions.answer: Exhaustively trying all combinations of hyperparameter values from predefined lists. Evaluates each combination via cross-validation and selects best performing.

questions.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of grid search?
questions.answer: Advantages: thorough, guaranteed to find best in grid, simple. Disadvantages: computationally expensive (exponential in number of hyperparameters), doesn't scale well, wastes time on poor regions.

questions.question: How does random search differ from grid search?
questions.answer: Random search samples random combinations of hyperparameters instead of trying all. More efficient for high dimensions, can explore wider range, often finds good solutions faster than grid search.

questions.question: What is a learning rate in gradient descent?
questions.answer: Hyperparameter controlling step size in parameter updates during optimization. Determines how much to change weights based on computed gradients.

questions.question: What happens if the learning rate is too high?
questions.answer: Updates overshoot minimum, causing oscillation, divergence, or failure to converge. Loss may increase or fluctuate wildly instead of decreasing smoothly.

questions.question: What happens if the learning rate is too low?
questions.answer: Very slow convergence, requires many epochs. May get stuck in local minima or saddle points. Training takes unnecessarily long time and computational resources.

questions.question: What is learning rate decay?
questions.answer: Gradually reducing learning rate during training. Start with larger rate for fast progress, then decrease to fine-tune and converge. Common schedules: step decay, exponential decay, cosine annealing.

questions.question: What does ADAM stand for?
questions.answer: Adaptive Moment Estimation. It combines ideas from momentum (first moment) and RMSprop (second moment).

questions.question: What are the advantages of Adam over standard gradient descent?
questions.answer: Adaptive per-parameter learning rates, momentum for acceleration, efficient for sparse gradients, requires little tuning, combines benefits of AdaGrad and RMSprop, works well in practice.

questions.question: How does Adam adapt learning rates?
questions.answer: Maintains exponential moving averages of gradients (first moment, momentum) and squared gradients (second moment, variance). Uses these to scale learning rates per parameter adaptively.

questions.question: What are the key hyperparameters in Adam?
questions.answer: Learning rate α (typically 0.001), β₁ for first moment (typically 0.9), β₂ for second moment (typically 0.999), ε for numerical stability (typically 10⁻⁸).

questions.question: What is momentum in gradient descent?
questions.answer: Technique that accumulates a velocity vector in direction of consistent gradients, accelerating in persistent directions while dampening oscillations. Like a ball rolling downhill gaining speed.

questions.question: How does momentum help optimization?
questions.answer: Accelerates convergence in relevant directions, dampens oscillations in high-curvature directions, helps escape local minima and saddle points, smooths the optimization path.

questions.question: What is the momentum coefficient?
questions.answer: Hyperparameter (usually 0.9) controlling how much previous velocity influences current update. Higher values = more momentum, lower = closer to standard gradient descent.

questions.question: What does DBSCAN stand for?
questions.answer: Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise. A clustering algorithm that groups points based on density.

questions.question: How does DBSCAN differ from K-Means?
questions.answer: DBSCAN doesn't require specifying K, can find arbitrary-shaped clusters, identifies noise/outliers, density-based rather than distance-based, doesn't force every point into a cluster.

questions.question: What are the main parameters in DBSCAN?
questions.answer: ε (epsilon): maximum distance between two points to be neighbors. min_samples: minimum points in neighborhood to form a dense region. These define density threshold.

questions.question: What are core points, border points, and noise points in DBSCAN?
questions.answer: Core: has ≥ min_samples within ε distance. Border: in neighborhood of core point but not core itself. Noise: neither core nor border, outliers not assigned to any cluster.

questions.question: What is t-SNE used for?
questions.answer: Dimensionality reduction primarily for visualization, reducing high-dimensional data to 2D or 3D while preserving local structure. Excels at revealing clusters and patterns visually.

questions.question: How does t-SNE differ from PCA?
questions.answer: t-SNE: non-linear, preserves local structure, stochastic, primarily for visualization. PCA: linear, preserves global structure, deterministic, can be used for other tasks. t-SNE better for complex patterns.

questions.question: Why is t-SNE primarily used for visualization?
questions.answer: It's non-deterministic (different runs give different results), computationally expensive, doesn't preserve distances or density globally, can't transform new data directly, and creates artifacts.

questions.question: What is the perplexity parameter in t-SNE?
questions.answer: Perplexity balances local vs global structure, roughly interpreted as number of close neighbors for each point. Typical values: 5-50. Higher values focus more on global structure.

questions.question: What is Natural Language Processing?
questions.answer: Field of AI focused on enabling computers to understand, interpret, generate, and manipulate human language. Combines linguistics, computer science, and machine learning.

questions.question: What are some common NLP tasks?
questions.answer: Text classification, sentiment analysis, named entity recognition, machine translation, question answering, text summarization, speech recognition, language generation.

questions.question: Why is NLP challenging?
questions.answer: Language is ambiguous, context-dependent, has idioms and sarcasm, exhibits syntactic/semantic complexity, varies across domains and cultures, and contains implicit knowledge.

questions.question: What is the difference between syntax and semantics in NLP?
questions.answer: Syntax: grammatical structure, word order, sentence formation rules. Semantics: meaning of words and sentences, relationships between concepts. Both are needed for understanding.

questions.question: What is tokenization in NLP?
questions.answer: Breaking text into smaller units (tokens) such as words, subwords, or characters. First step in most NLP pipelines, converting text into processable units.

questions.question: What are the different levels of tokenization?
questions.answer: Character-level (individual characters), subword-level (BPE, WordPiece), word-level (split on spaces/punctuation), sentence-level (split into sentences).

questions.question: What is subword tokenization?
questions.answer: Breaking words into smaller meaningful units. Handles rare words, reduces vocabulary size, captures morphology (prefix/suffix). Examples: BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece. Used in modern transformers.

questions.question: Why is tokenization important in NLP?
questions.answer: Defines input units for models, affects vocabulary size, impacts model's ability to handle unknown words, influences computational efficiency, and affects how models understand language structure.

questions.question: What are word embeddings?
questions.answer: Dense vector representations of words in continuous space where semantically similar words are close together. Capture semantic and syntactic relationships through vector geometry.

questions.question: Why are word embeddings better than one-hot encoding for text?
questions.answer: Embeddings are dense (not sparse), capture semantic similarity, require fewer dimensions, enable arithmetic operations (king - man + woman ≈ queen), and generalize better.

questions.question: What is the distributional hypothesis in word embeddings?
questions.answer: 'Words that occur in similar contexts have similar meanings.' Embeddings learn from co-occurrence patterns, placing words used similarly close in vector space.

questions.question: Give examples of word embedding models.
questions.answer: Word2Vec (Skip-gram, CBOW), GloVe (Global Vectors), FastText (subword embeddings), ELMo (contextualized), BERT embeddings (contextual transformers).

questions.question: What is Word2Vec?
questions.answer: Neural network-based method for learning word embeddings from large text corpora. Uses shallow neural networks to predict words from context or vice versa.

questions.question: What are the two architectures of Word2Vec?
questions.answer: Skip-gram: predicts context words given target word. CBOW (Continuous Bag of Words): predicts target word from context words. Skip-gram better for rare words.

questions.question: Explain the Skip-gram model.
questions.answer: Given a target word, predict surrounding context words within a window. Trained to maximize probability of context words. Better for rare words and larger datasets.

questions.question: Explain the CBOW (Continuous Bag of Words) model.
questions.answer: Given context words (window around position), predict the target word in middle. Averages context word vectors. Faster to train, better for frequent words.

questions.question: What is computer vision?
questions.answer: Field of AI enabling computers to understand and interpret visual information from images and videos, mimicking human visual perception.

questions.question: What are common tasks in computer vision?
questions.answer: Image classification, object detection, semantic segmentation, instance segmentation, face recognition, pose estimation, image generation, video analysis, optical character recognition.

questions.question: Why are CNNs particularly effective for computer vision?
questions.answer: CNNs exploit spatial structure, use local receptive fields, share parameters (translation invariance), automatically learn hierarchical features from edges to complex objects, and are computationally efficient.

questions.question: What is the difference between image classification and object detection?
questions.answer: Classification: assign label to entire image. Detection: locate and classify multiple objects within image using bounding boxes. Detection is more complex, providing 'what' and 'where'.

questions.question: What is object detection?
questions.answer: Identifying what objects are present in an image and where they are located, typically represented by bounding boxes and class labels.

questions.question: How does object detection differ from image classification?
questions.answer: Classification: one label per image. Detection: multiple objects with locations (bounding boxes) and labels per image. Detection requires both recognition and localization.

questions.question: What is a bounding box in object detection?
questions.answer: Rectangular box defined by coordinates (x, y, width, height) that encloses an object in the image. Used to localize detected objects.

questions.question: Name three popular object detection algorithms.
questions.answer: YOLO (You Only Look Once), R-CNN family (R-CNN, Fast R-CNN, Faster R-CNN), SSD (Single Shot Detector), RetinaNet, EfficientDet.

questions.question: What does YOLO stand for?
questions.answer: You Only Look Once. Emphasizes that it processes the entire image in a single forward pass, unlike multi-stage detectors.

questions.question: What makes YOLO fast for object detection?
questions.answer: Single-stage detector that processes entire image at once, no region proposals, unified architecture, real-time inference. Trades some accuracy for significant speed improvements.

questions.question: How does YOLO approach object detection differently from R-CNN?
questions.answer: YOLO: single-stage, looks at entire image once, predicts all boxes simultaneously. R-CNN: two-stage, region proposals then classification, slower but potentially more accurate.

questions.question: What problem does ResNet solve?
questions.answer: Solves the degradation problem where very deep networks perform worse than shallower ones due to vanishing gradients and optimization difficulties. Enables training of extremely deep networks (100+ layers).

questions.question: What are residual connections (skip connections)?
questions.answer: Direct connections that skip one or more layers, adding the input to the output: F(x) + x. Allows gradients to flow directly backward, enabling training of very deep networks.

questions.question: How do residual connections help with the vanishing gradient problem?
questions.answer: They create shortcut paths for gradients to flow backward without attenuation. Even if layer weights are small, gradients can flow through skip connections unimpeded.

questions.question: What does the number in ResNet-50 or ResNet-101 represent?
questions.answer: The total number of layers in the network. ResNet-50 has 50 layers, ResNet-101 has 101 layers. Deeper variants capture more complex features.

questions.question: What is data augmentation?
questions.answer: Creating new training examples by applying transformations to existing data. Artificially expands dataset size and diversity without collecting new data.

questions.question: Why is data augmentation useful?
questions.answer: Reduces overfitting, improves generalization, increases effective dataset size, makes models robust to variations, reduces need for more labeled data, acts as regularization.

questions.question: Give five examples of image augmentation techniques.
questions.answer: Rotation, flipping (horizontal/vertical), cropping/scaling, color jittering (brightness, contrast, saturation), translation, shearing, adding noise, cutout/random erasing.

questions.question: When should you apply data augmentation?
questions.answer: During training (on-the-fly or offline), not on validation/test sets. Use when you have limited data, experiencing overfitting, or want models robust to transformations.

questions.question: What is class imbalance?
questions.answer: When classes in dataset have significantly different frequencies. One class (majority) has many more examples than other(s) (minority). Common in fraud detection, medical diagnosis.

questions.question: Why is accuracy not a good metric for imbalanced datasets?
questions.answer: A model predicting only majority class can have high accuracy while being useless. E.g., 99% non-fraud: predicting all non-fraud gives 99% accuracy but catches no fraud.

questions.question: What techniques can address class imbalance?
questions.answer: Resampling (oversample minority, undersample majority), SMOTE, class weights, different metrics (F1, precision-recall), ensemble methods, anomaly detection approaches, cost-sensitive learning.

questions.question: What is the difference between oversampling and undersampling?
questions.answer: Oversampling: duplicate or synthesize minority class examples to balance classes. Undersampling: remove majority class examples. Oversampling risks overfitting, undersampling loses information.

questions.question: What does SMOTE stand for?
questions.answer: Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique. An oversampling method that creates synthetic examples rather than duplicating existing ones.

questions.question: How does SMOTE generate synthetic samples?
questions.answer: For each minority sample, finds k nearest neighbors, randomly selects one, and creates new sample along the line between them: x_new = x + λ(x_neighbor - x) where λ ∈ [0,1].

questions.question: What are the advantages of SMOTE over random oversampling?
questions.answer: Creates new synthetic examples rather than exact copies, reduces overfitting, increases diversity, creates more general decision boundaries, interpolates rather than duplicates.

questions.question: What is anomaly detection?
questions.answer: Identifying rare items, events, or observations that differ significantly from the majority of data. Also called outlier detection or novelty detection.

questions.question: Give three real-world applications of anomaly detection.
questions.answer: Fraud detection (credit card transactions), network intrusion detection, medical diagnosis (disease detection), manufacturing defect detection, system health monitoring.

questions.question: What makes anomaly detection challenging?
questions.answer: Anomalies are rare (imbalanced), definition of 'normal' evolves, anomalies can be subtle, labeling is expensive, high false positive cost, diverse types of anomalies.

questions.question: What is the difference between supervised and unsupervised anomaly detection?
questions.answer: Supervised: has labeled anomalies for training, essentially classification. Unsupervised: learns normal patterns, flags deviations as anomalies without labels. Unsupervised more common due to labeling difficulty.

questions.question: What is time series data?
questions.answer: Sequence of data points indexed in time order. Observations are dependent on time with temporal patterns like trends, seasonality, and autocorrelation.

questions.question: What are the components of a time series?
questions.answer: Trend (long-term direction), Seasonality (repeating patterns), Cyclical (longer irregular cycles), Irregular/Noise (random fluctuations). Often decomposed: Y = Trend + Seasonal + Residual.

questions.question: What is stationarity in time series?
questions.answer: Statistical properties (mean, variance) remain constant over time. Stationary series have no trend or seasonality. Many models require stationarity, achieved through differencing or detrending.

questions.question: Why is the order of observations important in time series?
questions.answer: Temporal dependencies exist - past influences future. Order matters for autocorrelation, causality, forecasting. Shuffling destroys temporal structure unlike i.i.d. data in most ML.

questions.question: What does BERT stand for?
questions.answer: Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers. Emphasizes bidirectional context understanding.

questions.question: What is masked language modeling in BERT?
questions.answer: Training objective where random tokens are masked, and model predicts them using bidirectional context. E.g., 'The cat [MASK] on mat' → predict 'sat'. Enables bidirectional learning.

questions.question: What makes BERT bidirectional?
questions.answer: Uses entire context (left and right) simultaneously when encoding each token, unlike GPT which only uses left context. Achieved through masked language modeling during pre-training.

questions.question: How is BERT typically used in practice?
questions.answer: Pre-trained on large corpus, then fine-tuned on specific tasks (classification, NER, QA). Transfer learning approach - leverages learned language understanding for downstream tasks.

questions.question: What does GPT stand for?
questions.answer: Generative Pre-trained Transformer. Emphasizes its generative capability and pre-training approach.

questions.question: What is autoregressive language modeling?
questions.answer: Predicting next token based on previous tokens: P(token_t | token_1, ..., token_{t-1}). Generates text sequentially left-to-right. Foundation of GPT's generative capability.

questions.question: How does GPT differ from BERT in terms of training objective?
questions.answer: GPT: autoregressive, predicts next word from left context only, causal attention. BERT: masked LM, uses full bidirectional context, masked attention. GPT better for generation, BERT for understanding.

questions.question: Why is GPT particularly good at text generation?
questions.answer: Autoregressive training directly optimizes for sequential generation. Each token generation is conditioned on previous tokens, naturally suited for coherent text production.

questions.question: What is explainable AI (XAI)?
questions.answer: Methods and techniques to make AI model decisions interpretable and understandable to humans. Provides transparency into how and why models make predictions.

questions.question: Why is model interpretability important?
questions.answer: Trust and transparency, regulatory compliance (GDPR), debugging models, ethical considerations, domain validation, identifying biases, safety-critical applications.

questions.question: What is the trade-off between model complexity and interpretability?
questions.answer: Simple models (linear, trees) are interpretable but less powerful. Complex models (deep learning) are powerful but black-box. Often must choose between accuracy and interpretability.

questions.question: Name three techniques for explaining model predictions.
questions.answer: SHAP (Shapley values), LIME (local approximations), feature importance, attention weights, saliency maps, partial dependence plots, counterfactual explanations.

questions.question: What does SHAP stand for?
questions.answer: SHapley Additive exPlanations. Based on Shapley values from cooperative game theory.

questions.question: What are Shapley values?
questions.answer: From game theory - fair distribution of gains from cooperation. For ML: contribution of each feature to prediction by considering all possible feature combinations.

questions.question: How does SHAP explain model predictions?
questions.answer: Assigns each feature an importance value (SHAP value) for a specific prediction. Positive values push prediction higher, negative lower. Sum of SHAP values equals prediction - baseline.

questions.question: What are the advantages of SHAP over other interpretability methods?
questions.answer: Theoretically grounded (Shapley values), consistent, local accuracy, model-agnostic, provides both local and global explanations, handles feature interactions.

questions.question: What is model compression and why is it important?
questions.answer: Reducing model size and computational requirements while maintaining performance. Important for deployment on edge devices, mobile, reducing latency, energy efficiency, cost savings.

questions.question: What are the main techniques for model compression?
questions.answer: Pruning (removing weights/neurons), Quantization (reducing precision), Knowledge distillation (training smaller model), Low-rank factorization, Neural architecture search.

questions.question: What is the trade-off in model compression?
questions.answer: Compression vs accuracy - reducing model size often decreases performance. Goal is finding optimal balance: maximum compression with acceptable accuracy loss.

questions.question: What is model quantization?
questions.answer: Reducing numerical precision of weights and activations from 32-bit float to lower precision (16-bit, 8-bit, or binary). Decreases memory and computation.

questions.question: How does quantization reduce model size?
questions.answer: Lower precision requires fewer bits per parameter. 32-bit→8-bit gives 4× compression. Reduces memory footprint, bandwidth, and enables faster integer operations.

questions.question: What is the difference between post-training quantization and quantization-aware training?
questions.answer: Post-training: quantize after training, quick but may lose accuracy. Quantization-aware: simulates quantization during training, model adapts, better accuracy but more complex.

questions.question: What is knowledge distillation?
questions.answer: Training a smaller 'student' model to mimic a larger 'teacher' model's behavior. Student learns from teacher's soft predictions, not just hard labels.

questions.question: What are teacher and student models in knowledge distillation?
questions.answer: Teacher: large, accurate, pre-trained model. Student: smaller, efficient model being trained. Student learns to approximate teacher's outputs and internal representations.

questions.question: What are soft targets in knowledge distillation?
questions.answer: Teacher's probability distributions over classes (softmax outputs with temperature). Provide richer information than hard labels - showing relative similarities between classes.

questions.question: What is semi-supervised learning?
questions.answer: Learning from both labeled and unlabeled data. Leverages small amount of labeled data with large amount of unlabeled data to improve performance.

questions.question: When is semi-supervised learning useful?
questions.answer: When labeling is expensive/time-consuming but unlabeled data is abundant. Medical imaging, speech recognition, web classification - where expert labeling is costly.

questions.question: How does semi-supervised learning use unlabeled data?
questions.answer: Learns data structure from unlabeled data (clustering, density), pseudo-labeling (model labels unlabeled data), consistency regularization, co-training with multiple views.

questions.question: What is self-supervised learning?
questions.answer: Learning from unlabeled data by creating supervised tasks automatically from the data itself. Labels are derived from data structure, not human annotation.

questions.question: What is a pretext task in self-supervised learning?
questions.answer: Automatically created auxiliary task used for pre-training. Not the end goal but teaches useful representations. Examples: predicting rotation, colorization, next word prediction.

questions.question: Give examples of pretext tasks used in self-supervised learning.
questions.answer: Vision: rotation prediction, jigsaw puzzles, colorization, inpainting. NLP: masked language modeling, next sentence prediction. Audio: temporal ordering, speed prediction.

questions.question: What is few-shot learning?
questions.answer: Learning to classify new categories from very few labeled examples (typically 1-5 per class). Mimics human ability to learn from limited examples.

questions.question: What is the difference between few-shot and zero-shot learning?
questions.answer: Few-shot: has few labeled examples of new classes. Zero-shot: no examples of new classes, relies on auxiliary information (descriptions, attributes). Zero-shot is more challenging.

questions.question: Why is few-shot learning challenging?
questions.answer: Insufficient data to learn complex patterns, high risk of overfitting, difficult to generalize from few examples, requires learning good representations and similarity metrics.

questions.question: What is meta-learning?
questions.answer: Learning how to learn. Training models to quickly adapt to new tasks using experience from multiple related tasks. Learns learning algorithms themselves.

questions.question: What does 'learning to learn' mean in meta-learning?
questions.answer: Instead of learning one specific task, learning the process of learning itself - how to quickly adapt to new tasks with minimal data. Learns good initialization, learning strategies, or representations.

questions.question: How does meta-learning differ from traditional machine learning?
questions.answer: Traditional ML: train on one task from scratch. Meta-learning: trains across many tasks to learn adaptation strategy, enabling quick learning on new tasks.

questions.question: What is online learning in machine learning?
questions.answer: Learning incrementally from streaming data, updating model as new data arrives. Data comes sequentially, processed once, then discarded. Continuous adaptation.

questions.question: How does online learning differ from batch learning?
questions.answer: Batch: trains on entire fixed dataset at once, periodic retraining. Online: processes data sequentially as it arrives, continuous updates, adapts to changing distributions.

questions.question: When is online learning preferred over batch learning?
questions.answer: When data is too large to fit in memory, data arrives continuously (streams), distribution changes over time (concept drift), need real-time adaptation, computational constraints.

questions.question: What is active learning?
questions.answer: Interactive learning where model selects most informative examples to label. Human labels only selected examples. Reduces labeling effort while maintaining performance.

questions.question: What is a query strategy in active learning?
questions.answer: Method for selecting which unlabeled examples to label next. Strategies: uncertainty sampling (most uncertain), query by committee, expected model change, diversity-based.

questions.question: Why is active learning useful when labeling is expensive?
questions.answer: Achieves same performance with fewer labeled examples by intelligently selecting most informative samples. Reduces annotation cost and time while maintaining or improving model quality.

questions.question: What is federated learning?
questions.answer: Distributed learning where model trains across decentralized devices/servers holding local data. Data never leaves devices - only model updates are shared.

questions.question: How does federated learning preserve privacy?
questions.answer: Raw data stays on local devices, never centralized. Only model parameters/gradients shared. Enables learning from sensitive data (medical, personal) without exposing it.

questions.question: What are the challenges of federated learning?
questions.answer: Communication costs, heterogeneous data distributions (non-IID), device heterogeneity, unreliable connections, privacy attacks on gradients, convergence with partial participation.

questions.question: What is a Gaussian Mixture Model?
questions.answer: Probabilistic model assuming data comes from mixture of multiple Gaussian distributions. Each cluster is a Gaussian with its own mean and covariance.

questions.question: How do GMMs differ from K-Means?
questions.answer: GMM: probabilistic, soft assignments, elliptical clusters, models uncertainty. K-Means: hard assignments, spherical clusters, deterministic. GMM more flexible but computationally expensive.

questions.question: What is the Expectation-Maximization algorithm used for in GMMs?
questions.answer: EM iteratively estimates GMM parameters. E-step: compute probability of each point belonging to each cluster. M-step: update cluster parameters based on soft assignments. Repeat until convergence.

questions.question: What is early stopping?
questions.answer: Regularization technique that stops training when validation performance stops improving. Prevents overfitting by finding optimal training duration.

questions.question: How does early stopping prevent overfitting?
questions.answer: Monitors validation error during training. Stops before model starts overfitting (when validation error increases while training error decreases). Finds sweet spot of generalization.

questions.question: What metric is typically monitored for early stopping?
questions.answer: Validation loss or relevant validation metric (accuracy, F1, etc.). Often uses patience parameter - stops if no improvement for N epochs.

questions.question: What is semantic segmentation?
questions.answer: Pixel-wise classification - assigning a class label to every pixel in an image. Creates dense prediction map showing what object each pixel belongs to.

questions.question: How does semantic segmentation differ from object detection?
questions.answer: Detection: bounding boxes around objects. Segmentation: precise pixel-level boundaries. Segmentation provides exact shape, detection provides approximate location.

questions.question: What is pixel-wise classification?
questions.answer: Classifying each individual pixel in an image independently. Every pixel gets a label indicating what object/class it belongs to. Produces dense semantic map.

questions.question: What is U-Net architecture designed for?
questions.answer: Biomedical image segmentation, though now widely used for general segmentation. U-shaped architecture with encoder-decoder and skip connections.

questions.question: What are skip connections in U-Net?
